turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I tried building an lru crate using a HashMap to store values and a LinkedList for order, but my capacity check keeps failing. It seems like the eviction logic isn't triggering when I think it should.

The confusion usually comes from how you iterate over the LinkedList while simultaneously mutating the HashMap. In Rust, borrowing rules prevent you from holding a reference to one collection and modifying another if they share data without care. You need to ensure your eviction logic runs before you try to read the full capacity check, otherwise the cache state is inconsistent during that window.
I see, so I shouldn't be iterating while holding references? But my LinkedList is just a VecDeque and HashMap is just a HashMap. Why isn't that enough?

The issue is that your eviction logic must run before you attempt to insert or access the cache state. If you check capacity after adding an item, the count might already exceed limits in memory even if logically it shouldn't yet be removed. You need a method that atomically removes the tail of the LinkedList and updates the HashMap keys before proceeding with new inserts.
I'm back in the game after five years away, but I keep hitting a wall with this new codebase. Why does my cache implementation even work this way when I thought it was broken?

It's not actually broken; you're probably relying on the lru crate for thread safety while trying to build your own manual version with a LinkedList and HashMap. The real issue is that a standard LinkedList plus HashMap isn't thread-safe by default, so if you're using Arc<LinkedList> without proper locking or a Mutex wrapper, concurrent reads will panic. The lru crate solves this because it's built from the ground up to be safe in multi-threaded environments.
So I can't just use Arc and Mutex on my own LinkedList if I want to avoid deadlocks?

You have two options here. First, you could implement your own thread-safe LRU using a Ring buffer with atomic operations or a specialized lock-free structure like the one found in std::sync::Mutex but optimized for eviction patterns. Second—and easier—you should just use the lru crate which wraps your LinkedList and HashMap internally with proper synchronization logic so you don't have to reason about ownership transfers between threads manually.
I've been following that blog post on cache optimization and I thought the lru crate just wraps a simple HashMap for me to look up by key, so why do they mention needing a LinkedList at all?

Actually, the lru crate doesn't just wrap a HashMap because an LRU policy requires tracking usage order which a HashMap alone can't provide. The crate internally combines a HashMap with a doubly-linked list where the head represents the most recently accessed item and the tail is the least recently used one.
But if I'm using Rust, shouldn't I just use an Arc<HashSet> or something similar to handle concurrency like in my Go library? Why do they insist on manual eviction logic?

The confusion often comes from thinking collections are just storage, but an LRU cache needs both fast lookup and strict access order tracking. The lru crate provides a thread-safe wrapper that handles the synchronization internally so you don't need to manually manage Arc or mutexes around your LinkedList and HashMap.
I still think I can implement this myself with just two HashMaps—one for keys and another for timestamps—to track usage order without the extra complexity of an LRU crate.

While you could store a timestamp alongside each key, Rust's standard library doesn't provide a built-in ordered list type like C++ does, so implementing manual eviction with LinkedList requires careful handling of interior mutability and synchronization. The lru crate abstracts away these complexities by providing a single thread-safe collection that handles both the HashMap lookups and the linked-list ordering automatically.
Okay, but what happens if I try to use this in a multi-threaded context where multiple workers are trying to update the cache at once? Will the eviction logic still hold up?

That's exactly why the lru crate is designed with thread safety in mind—it wraps the internal LinkedList and HashMap with proper locking mechanisms so concurrent updates don't corrupt your data structure. When an item hits capacity limits, it safely performs the eviction by removing from both the tail of the linked list and the map without race conditions.

I see, but what about performance? If I'm doing millions of requests per second, will the overhead of maintaining that doubly-linked list structure slow down my application compared to just using a simple HashMap?

The lru crate is optimized for this exact scenario because it uses a hybrid approach where the HashMap handles O(1) lookups while the linked list only incurs minimal overhead during cache misses or evictions. For high-throughput applications, you'll notice significantly better performance than manually managing LinkedList and HashMap synchronization yourself.
